content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
March 15, 1990
PauI Koplin
Executive Director
The California Channel
Third Floor, IO95I W. Pico BLvd.
Los Angeles, CA 900064
Re:
Your Request For Advice
our File No. A-90-121
Dear Mr. Koplin:
You have requested advice under the lobbying disclosure
provisions of the Polj,tical Reform Act (the Act) .1/
QUESTTON
Does certain activity The California Channel proposes to
engage in meet the definition of lobbying activity which would
require its enployees to register and file disclosure reports as
Iobbyists, or which would require The California ChanneL to
register and file reports as a lobbyist employer?
CONCLUSION
The activities 
described in your letter neet the definition
of influencing or attempting to influence legislative action.
Therefore, if any employee of The California Channel engages in
these activities 
to the extent that the thresholds for qualifying
as a lobbyist are met, the ernployee will neet the definition of a
ttlobbyist,I and must file disclosure reports. 
If any enployee of
The California channel qualifies as a lobbyist, The california
L/
Government Code Sections 81000-91015. A1l statutory
references are to the GovernEent code unless otherwise indicated.
Comnission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. AIl references to regulations are to
Title 2, Division 6 of the California code of Regulations.
.l2S J Street, Suite 8OO . P.O. Box 8O7 o Sacramento CA 95tto4-O8tl7 . (916) ll:-;660


Paul Koplin
Page frro
Channel must register and file disclosure reports as a lobbyist
employer - In addition, even in the event that no individuat
enployee qualifies as a lobbyist, if The California Channel spends
$5,000 or more in a calendar quarter in connection with the
described activities, 
it would be required to file reports
disclosing the pa)rnents.
ANALYSIS
The Act defines rrinfluencing tegislative actionr as follows:
t'Inf luencing legislative or adrninistrativer neans
promoting, supporting, influencing, modifying, opposing
or delaying any legislative or adrninistrative action by
any means, including but not Lirnited to the provisions
or use of infornation, statistics, 
studies or analyses.
(Section 82032.)
ttlegislative actionrr neans:
The drafting, introduction, consideration, modification,
enactment or defeat of any biII, resolution, arnendment,
report, nomination or other matter by the Legislature or
by- eitirer house or iltEnnitte-e,-sulcomrnitt6J, joint
or select comrnittee thereof, or by a nenber or eurployee
of the Legislature acting in his otticiat capacity. -
ItLegislative actiontt also means the action of the-
covernor in approving or vetoing any bil1.
(Section a2O37 . Emphasis Added.)
- As you can see, the Act,s definitions of r'Iegislative actj.on"
and "influencing legislative action, are very broid, and include
attenpting to influence a legislative officiil 
to ,,consid.er, r in
addition to a rrbill, resolution, amendment, report, or
nonination, rr any nother matter.i
_ 
Therefore, even though rhe california channel,s contacts with
legislative officials do not involve any pending ". p."p"r"a
legisration, . the purpose of the contact3 is to f,rovii"-ini"..uti""
upon which, it is hoped, the Legislature wiII bise i-a""i=i""
which wil] tead to a decision.t5 implernent the proposar lresentea
to the Legislature by The california Channel.
_.__ Il-1 formal opinion, the conmission provided that, in certain
crrcunsrances, contacts with legislative officials wouid not be
attempts to infruence regislatiie action. rn rn re cohen lrszsy
1 FPPC 10, the Commission concluded that the sElectionE ;


PauI Koplin
Page Three
contractor by individual legislators for the rnailing of their
Iegislative neusletters would not constitute legislative action
because each individual legislator vras able to select the
contractor for his or her own newsletter. The situation discussed
in this Letter is distinguishable from the situation addressed in
gghen, however, because the decision to pernit videotaping of
Iegislative proceedings would be made by the Legislature as a
body, or by a comrnittee of the Legislature.
You also have asked us whether the exenption in Section
86300(b) applies to The California channel. Section 863o0(b)
provides that
the lobbying provisions contained in Chapter 6 of the Act do not
apply to:
Any ne\,rspaper or other periodical of general circulation,
book publisher, radio or television station (including
any individual who owns, publishes, or is enployed by any
such newspaper or periodical, radio or television station)
which in the ordinary course of business publishes news
items, editorials, or other conments, or paid advertise-
nent, whj.ch directly or indirectly urge legislative or
adninistrative action if such newspaper, periodical, book
publisher, radio or television station or individual,
engages in no further or other activities 
in connection
with urging legislative or aduinistrative action other
than to appear before a committee of the Legislature or
before a state agency in support or in opposition to
such action. . . .
This exemption does not apply to The California channel
for the following reasons:
(1) The activities 
which The California ChanneL proposes to
engage in involve trneeting with legislators to discuss the
report,s recornmendations and seeking their support for the concept
of televising legislative sessions.I This activity does not meet
the standard set out in Section 8630o(b) for activities which are
exempt. The exenption set out in Section 86300(b) applies only to
rrnews items, editorials, or other comments, or paid advertisement"
which are published rrin the normal course of buliness.,t
.(21 The California Channel does not appear to meet the
definition of tra newspaper, or other periodiLal of general
circulation, book publisher, radio or television stition.',
You have indicated that, if The California Channel is
successful in its efforts to convince the Legisrature to instarl
and operate remote controrred cameras, and the california channel
were to gain access to the.video signal and, therefore, be able to
broadcast legisrative hearings, it wourd become a ,noncommerciaL
tel-evision network- I' At the point The California Channei ictually


PauI Koplin
Page Four
becones a television station, the news iteus, editorials, or other
comments, or paid advertisements it broadcasts in the normal
course of business rrould not rneet the definition of rinfluencing
legislative action,r as specifically exenpted by Section 863o0(b).
In summary, the activities described in your letter, i.e.,
meetings with legislators to discuss The California Channelrs
recornnendations and to seek legislative support for televising
legislative sessions, meet the definition of rrinfluencj.ng
legisJ.ative action. rt
If any individual employee of The California channel. meets
one of the qualification tests set out in Regulation 18239 (copy
enclosed), The California Channel must register and file reports
as a lobbyist employer, and the ernployee nust file reports as a
lobbyist. (Sections 86113, 861I5 (a) . )
If no employee meets the definition of a lobbyist, but The
California Channel spends $5,000 or nore in a calendar quarter, it
must file reports pursuant to Section 86115(b).
Enclosed for your infornation is the Commission,s
t'Infornation Manual On Lobbying Provisions Of The Political Reform
Act.rr Please contact the Comrnission,s Technical Assistance and
Analysis Division at (916) 322-5662 if you have questions
concerning the Act,s lobbying registration and reporting
provisions.
If you have any questions about this letter, please call me
at (9I5) 322-s562.
S incerely,
Kathryn E. Donovan
輛
eジ獅
By: Jeanne Pritchard
Division Chief
Technical Assistance and
Analysis Division
